en: Yeah, I've always loved really big scary goals. I think I'm not someone who's scared of failing.
zh: 是的，我一直都很喜歡那些又大又嚇人的目標。我覺得我不是一個害怕失敗的人。
speaker: Guest

en: >> How can we  learn not to be scared of failing?
zh: 我們要怎麼學會不害怕失敗呢？
speaker: Host

en: >> I'm like willing  to accept that I might fail at this, but it's pushing me out of my comfort zone, and that can only be a good thing. If I have to end my career here, winning this race, I'm at peace with that as long as I can get the win.
zh: 我願意接受自己可能會失敗，但這件事正在把我推出舒適圈，而那只會是一件好事。如果我必須在這裡結束我的職業生涯，贏得這場比賽，只要我能拿下勝利，我就心安了。
speaker: Guest

en: Then to do that four times and still be second and still be like, "No, I still want this. I'm still going to come back. I'm still too stubborn to step away and be satisfied."
zh: 然後經歷了四次都還是第二名，但仍然會說：「不，我還是想要。我還是會回來。我太固執了，不會就這樣放手然後感到滿足。」
speaker: Guest

en: Someone's  like, "That was mental." I'm like, "Yes, I absolutely smashed it."
zh: 有人說：「那太瘋狂了。」我說：「是啊，我完全發揮到極致了。」
speaker: Guest

speaker: Guest

I mean, you're probably still on the bike first, I think, because of uh of me losing my helmet or something along those lines. But is that your final excuse then, is it?
zh: 所以那就是你最終的藉口嗎？因為在那場比賽之前，我一直聽到有個女選手叫 Lucy，她會在游泳賽段把我甩得遠遠的。然後你確實游了出來。我的意思是，你可能在自行車賽段還是第一個出發的，因為我好像掉了頭盔什麼的。但那就是你最終的藉口了嗎？

speaker: Guest

zh: 我必須說，這些年來我對你最欣賞的一點就是你總是充滿雄心壯志，你總是有遠大而勇敢的目標。你會去追求它們，而且你很少會說「我就隨波逐流吧」。在目標設定方面，你心裡是怎麼想的？大家都在說⋯⋯

zh: 是的，我一直都喜歡非常大、令人害怕的目標。我覺得我不是那種害怕失敗的人。比如我現在有一個相當大的目標，我有百分之九十九的把握自己會失敗，但這並不妨礙我為此拼命努力。

speaker: Guest

speaker: Guest

speaker: Guest

speaker: Guest

speaker: Guest

speaker: Guest

en: mean, running is a little bit more difficult because it's weightbearing and I feel a bit swimmer heavy at the moment. But I'm sure that that's going to transition into running well in the back end of the year. But yeah, like I

speaker: Guest

en: said, I think it's not being scared of failing. And I never really have been. and I'm like willing to accept that I might fail at this, but it's pushing me out of my comfort zone and that can only
zh: 但就像我說的，我覺得關鍵是不要害怕失敗。我從來都不害怕。我願意接受自己可能會失敗，但它正在推動我走出舒適區，而那只會帶來好處。
speaker: Guest

en: be a good thing. >> How can we learn not to be scared of failing? Like personally, I have to say that's probably one of my absolute pet fears. Like I I only raced very few times throughout my career cuz I always wanted to be ready. And if I'm very honest, I look back, I always had a fear of failure. Like even starting this podcast project, starting anything new, there's always that self- expectation, the expectation from the outside. And
zh: 這只會是一件好事。我們要怎麼學會不害怕失敗呢？就我個人而言，我必須說那大概是我最大的恐懼之一。我整個職業生涯只比了很少幾場比賽，因為我總是想要準備好才上場。如果我非常誠實地回顧，我一直都有對失敗的恐懼。就像開始做這個 Podcast 節目，開始任何新事物，總是有自我期望，還有外界的期望。
speaker: Host

en: here you are saying you've never been afraid of failing. How did you get there? And and and how do you think you know that that's a skill you can forge?
zh: 而你卻說你從來不害怕失敗。你是怎麼做到的？你覺得這是一種可以鍛鍊出來的能力嗎？
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah, it's I mean it's difficult. I've failed at a lot of things in my life, but I've also been incredibly successful others. And I think I haven't appreciated the success I've had without having to fail first. It's made the success mean so much more.
zh: 嗯，這確實很難。我人生中失敗過很多次，但在其他方面也取得了非常大的成功。我覺得如果沒有先經歷失敗，我就不會那麼珍惜自己取得的成功。失敗讓成功變得更有意義。
speaker: Guest

en: >> I mean, my biggest failure was probably what happened in Kona last year and the whole it felt like the whole world was watching. So, I guess that really allowed me to be a bit at ease with it because I was like, well, everyone kind of witnessed that and they haven't turned their back on me and been like, oh, she's a failure. They're like, okay, well, things go wrong even at the highest level, even when you think you've done the perfect preparation. So,
zh: 我最大的失敗大概是去年在 Kona 發生的事，感覺好像全世界都在看。所以，我想那反而讓我能比較坦然地面對，因為我想：好吧，大家都目睹了，但他們沒有因此背棄我，說「她是個失敗者」。他們反而會說：好吧，即使在最高水平，即使你認為做了完美的準備，事情還是可能出差錯。
speaker: Guest

en: yeah, it's going to happen. And I think it's just being at peace with that and knowing that on the other side of failure is probably a success. And that's definitely what happened to me. Marba, I kind of was able to turn it around and winning the 70.3 worlds meant so much more because of what had happened in Kona. So yeah, trying to be at peace with it, but it's it's not always easy. I don't like failing, but yeah, it's inevitable it probably will happen.
zh: 所以，失敗是會發生的。我覺得重要的是與它和平共處，並且知道在失敗的另一面很可能就是成功。這也確實是發生在我身上的事。在 Marbella，我成功扭轉了局面，贏得 70.3 世錦賽冠軍，而那次勝利因為 Kona 的經歷而更加意義重大。所以，試著坦然接受它吧，雖然這並不總是容易的。我不喜歡失敗，但失敗很可能是無法避免的。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Yeah, I think it's always that thing you have to go through to actually grow, right? and you've done it very publicly always. I mean, the eyes are on you. You've created um an image inside the sport that probably supersedes the sport and people are watching all the time. Do you think that the outside support is the thing that lifts you up in those times when people don't turn? zh: 是的，我覺得失敗總是你必須經歷的事情，才能真正成長，對吧？而且你一直都是在公眾面前經歷這一切。大家的目光一直在你身上。你在這項運動中建立了一個可能超越運動本身的形象，人們一直在關注你。你覺得外界的支持是在那些時刻把你撐起來的力量嗎？因為我覺得另一方面，你也比任何進入這項運動的人受到更嚴苛的評判。
speaker: Host

en: generally go about and we think how is someone being celebrated for coming fifth and you're being you people tie rocks to your feet for coming second. You know it was like the old it's been talked about plenty but the forever rides made um kind of analogy that the media has maybe put on you at times. How do you deal with that balance? you know, how people Yeah. lift you up and and and then in a moment of success also don't cherish it as much as they probably could.
zh: 你知道我們通常會怎麼想——怎麼有人拿了第五名還被慶祝，而你拿了第二名卻被人在腳上綁石頭。就像以前那個——已經被討論過很多次了——Forever Rides 節目中媒體有時給你貼上的那種標籤。你怎麼處理這種落差？人們一方面把你捧起來，但在你成功的時刻，卻沒有像他們應該做的那樣去珍視它。
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah, it's it's definitely a challenge. Like I think the nature of the way that I race, I'm always out front because of the swim. So the cameras are always on me. So every mistake I made is seen. So,
zh: 是的，這絕對是個挑戰。我覺得因為我比賽的方式，游泳之後我總是在最前面，所以鏡頭一直對著我。所以我犯的每一個錯誤都被看到了。
speaker: Guest

en: I do feel like at times I'm held to a higher standard than other athletes who aren't on the camera and probably are making the same mistakes that I just did, but I'm the one that's going to be criticized. And it always looks like I'm doing things wrong. And the only
zh: 我確實覺得有時候我被用比其他選手更高的標準來檢視，因為那些不在鏡頭前的選手可能也犯了跟我一樣的錯誤，但被批評的人是我。看起來總是我在做錯事。
speaker: Guest

en: positive I take from that is like I'm going to then go away and work on those things maybe more than someone else who it wasn't picked up upon. Sometimes I don't worry too much about them because I'm like at the end of the day it's an iron man. the margins aren't as much as maybe doing an Olympic distance triathlon and I've done one of those and that was incredibly tough and I felt like I really did focus in more on those margins and doing things right. But
zh: 唯一讓我從中得到正面收穫的是，我會去針對那些問題加以改進，可能比其他沒被發現問題的人更努力。有時候我也不太擔心那些事，因為我想說到底這是鐵人賽，差距不像奧運距離鐵人三項那麼關鍵。我參加過一次奧運距離的比賽，那真的非常艱難，我覺得我確實更加注重那些細節和做對每件事。
speaker: Guest

en: yeah, I I have a very fixed skin. I think I've grown up in a world where I feel like I've always been judged, but I've always kind of put myself out there in a position to be judged and I accept that like if you have a platform that big, there's always going to be a percentage of people that don't like you or don't like what you're doing. and I don't really care to be honest. I'm happy doing what I'm doing and I'm chasing big goals. If you don't like it, then unfollow me.
zh: 但是，我的臉皮很厚。我覺得我成長在一個總是被評判的環境中，但我一直把自己放在會被評判的位置上，我也接受這一點。如果你有那麼大的平台，總會有一部分人不喜歡你或不喜歡你做的事。老實說我不在乎。我很開心做我正在做的事，我在追逐遠大的目標。如果你不喜歡，那就取消關注我吧。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Yes. Yes, definitely. That's uh it's a very good motto. If I can just push you on that a little bit though, your race tactic, as you said, always off the front, right? And you say you've worked on your weaknesses. you go out and at the same time you said you've probably not shown what you're capable of in a race in terms of you know all three disciplines specifically I guess your run do you have a fear of something else with your race tactic that you never
zh: 是的，是的，絕對是。那是一個非常好的座右銘。不過如果我可以再追問你一下，你的比賽戰術，就像你說的，總是從一開始就衝在前面，對吧？你也說你一直在加強自己的弱項。你出去比賽，同時你又說你可能還沒有在比賽中展現出你真正的實力，就三個項目來說，我猜特別是跑步的部分——你對自己的比賽戰術是否有某種擔憂，就是你從來沒有——
speaker: Host

en: actually allow yourself to get to the run in the kind of shape that you can show your form
zh: 真正讓自己以良好的狀態站上賽場，這樣你才能展現出應有的實力
speaker: Host

en: >> it's quite a good point like I think sometimes I don't race in the most tactically best way. I'm always just out there giving it 100% absolutely gunning it. And Reese is like, "Why didn't you let someone else take a turn or why didn't you?" And I'm like, "I just wanted to push it and see how far we could go." And it's like, "No, but you're racing now." It's not like in training where you want to get every ounce out of yourself. You need to be racing a bit more tactically smart. And I think sometimes I do just get carried away in the moment. Like I'm an athlete that absolutely loves racing and just giving it everything. And there's
zh: 這是個很好的觀點。我覺得有時候我的比賽方式確實不是戰術上最聰明的。我總是全力以赴、拼命往前衝。Reese 會說：「你為什麼不讓別人領一段？」我就說：「我只是想看看能推多遠。」他說：「不是，你現在是在比賽，不是訓練時要榨乾自己的每一分力氣，你需要比賽得更聰明一點。」我覺得有時候我就是在那個當下太投入了。我是一個超級熱愛比賽、全力付出的運動員。
speaker: Guest

en: definitely been races before where my tactic of just leading out and going works. And when I've had my best performances, that has worked. But I think the thing that I guess was a shame when I went into Kona and when I did win in 2023, we went into that race confident that I could win it from any angle. We believe that if I got caught on the bike, it wouldn't matter. I could run outun anyone. Like we went in with that position and then unfortunately I got injured on mile one of the marathon and it became a completely different struggle that day. But that also gives me a lot of positives because I feel
zh: 之前確實有些比賽，我領跑然後一路衝到底的策略是奏效的，當我表現最好的時候，這個方法確實管用。但我覺得比較可惜的是，當我去參加 Kona 並在 2023 年奪冠時，我們進入那場比賽時很有信心，覺得我可以從任何角度贏下比賽。我們相信就算我在自行車段被追上也沒關係，我可以跑贏任何人。我們帶著這樣的信心去參賽，但不幸的是我在馬拉松第一英里就受傷了，那天變成了一場完全不同的掙扎。但這也給了我很多正面的啟示，因為我覺得
speaker: Guest

en: like I could go and do that race better and that's why I'm gonna keep coming back. I think if I'd won it and had the most perfect day, maybe I could have just walked away from the sport and been like, done it. Yeah, can't do any better than that. But the thing that keeps me coming back is knowing that I haven't quite mastered the whole thing in one go to the best of my ability. And hopefully I'll be able to show that before I do step away from the sport. the forever
zh: 我覺得我可以回去把那場比賽跑得更好，這就是為什麼我會一直回來。我想如果當時我贏了，而且那天一切都完美，也許我就可以直接離開這項運動，覺得「做到了，不可能更好了」。但讓我不斷回來的動力，就是知道我還沒有在一場比賽中把所有環節都發揮到極致。希望在我退出這項運動之前，能夠展現出那樣的表現。
speaker: Guest

en: inner critic, always wanting more. Um, the the quote I found that really struck me about that race was one you gave after after the race saying, "You don't think any amount of pain would have come close to losing or coming second again." Um, with a torn calf. I mean, there's a lot there. What was going through your mind in those moments thinking you're here, you've done everything you can and now your body might not be able to put it together.
zh: 永遠的內心批評家，總是想要更多。我找到一段你賽後說的話，真的讓我印象深刻。你說：「我覺得再大的痛苦，都比不上再次輸掉或再拿第二名。」而且還是帶著撕裂的小腿肌。這裡面有太多東西了。在那些時刻，你腦子裡在想什麼？你已經到了那裡，你做了一切能做的準備，但現在你的身體可能無法撐到最後。
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah, I remember on that run thinking if I have to end my career here winning this race, I'm at peace with that as long as I can get the win. And for quite a while after the race when I went and saw experts, they were like, "You might not ever actually run again because of the severity of this um injury that you've caused yourself." And yeah, I was like, "Okay, well, but I got the win that I've wanted for so many years that I've been chasing. Maybe that's okay."
zh: 是的，我記得在跑步時心裡想著，如果我的職業生涯必須在這場比賽後結束，只要能贏下這場，我就心安了。賽後很長一段時間，我去看了專家，他們說：「因為你造成的傷勢太嚴重了，你可能再也無法跑步了。」而我當時想：「好吧，但我拿到了追逐多年的那場勝利，也許這樣就好了。」
speaker: Guest

en: Obviously, sat here now, I'm very grateful that it wasn't. I've had a lot of great performances since then. But it was probably one of the most challenging mental things I've ever had to overcome just in that amount of pain, but just telling yourself to just keep putting one foot in front of the other, but not actually knowing if your body's going to hold on. Like, am I going to make another stride and then that tear the calf just that little bit more that it's not going to be able to run anymore. Um,
zh: 當然，現在坐在這裡，我很感激結果不是那樣。之後我又有了很多出色的表現。但那可能是我經歷過最具挑戰性的心理考驗之一——在那樣的劇痛中，不斷告訴自己再邁出一步就好，但其實根本不知道身體能不能撐住。我會不會再跑一步，小腿就再撕裂一點，然後就完全跑不動了。
speaker: Guest

en: so it was quite scary. I remember the relief people saw on my face. Probably most of them assumed that was cuz I'd finally won on my fifth attempt. But the relief really was that I'd actually made it that far. I didn't think I was going to be able to run that full marathon. I knew mentally I would never stop, but it was physically am I going to end up crawling because this leg doesn't function anymore. And I mean
zh: 所以那真的很可怕。我記得人們看到我臉上的如釋重負，大多數人可能以為那是因為我終於在第五次嘗試中贏了。但那種釋然其實是因為我竟然真的撐到了終點。我以為自己跑不完整場馬拉松。我知道精神上我永遠不會停下來，但身體上，我會不會最後得用爬的，因為這條腿已經不能動了。
speaker: Guest

en: when we went and got it scanned, I actually went to it was a college of um people studying ultrasound scans and they were kind of at the end of their degrees and I was like a guinea pig that went in and they were scanning my leg and they were like, "But surely you didn't run with it like that. Like it's there's nothing left of it." And I was like, "Yeah, no, I I did. I I run a marathon. I think it was a 257 or something marathon with it like that." And yeah, their faces were pretty much horrified. Um, yeah. So, if I don't have
zh: 當我們去做掃描的時候，我去的其實是一所大學，裡面都是學習超音波掃描的學生，他們差不多快要畢業了，而我就像是進去給他們練手的白老鼠。他們掃描我的腿之後，整個人都嚇到了，說：「你該不會就這樣跑步吧？這裡面根本什麼都不剩了。」我就說：「對啊，沒錯，我真的跑了。我就這樣跑了一場馬拉松，好像是2小時57分之類的成績。」然後，他們的表情基本上就是一臉驚恐。嗯，對。所以，如果我沒有的話——
speaker: Guest

en: to go through that again, then that would be quite nice.
zh: 如果能再經歷一次那樣的事，那就太好了。
speaker: Guest

en: >> That would be nice. Yeah, you never know with an Iron Man. It's somehow it's never a pleasant experience, but uh at least not during the moment. But, um,
zh: 那確實會很好。是啊，鐵人賽你永遠不知道會怎樣。不知怎的，它從來都不是一次愉快的體驗，至少在比賽當下不是。但是⋯⋯
speaker: Host

en: where did this actually happen? Uh, like for those that didn't follow along, like did you were fit and healthy going into the race, right?
zh: 這到底是怎麼發生的？對於那些沒有一路關注的人來說，你賽前身體狀況是健康良好的，對吧？
speaker: Host

en: >> Yes. Going into the race, I was really fit and healthy. I was in great shape. I'd done a really easy run probably about one week before the race in Kona and I'd felt a little bit of a twinge, but it was only very small, so I didn't think too much of it. And I had my physio there and he was confident, it was fine. Um, and then yeah, I remember
zh: 是的，賽前我的身體狀態非常好，體能也很棒。大概在科納比賽前一週，我做了一次很輕鬆的跑步，感覺小腿有一點點不適，但非常輕微，所以我沒太在意。我的物理治療師也在場，他很有信心地說沒問題。
speaker: Guest

en: running out the swim and thinking it doesn't feel particularly good. Um, but got on the bike and I felt absolutely fine. I was having a great bike ride.
zh: 然後，我記得游完泳跑出水面時，感覺不太舒服。但上了自行車後我感覺完全沒問題，騎得非常順利。
speaker: Guest

en: And then when I ran round the pier in T2, I was like, doesn't feel that good, but I've cycled 112 mi. It never feels really good at this point. And then I remember running up the hill out of transition and you're on that first road before you make the right. I think it's down market street to go onto Alihi. And along that straight, I just felt the most agonizing pain in my calf and was like, "Oh, this is really, really bad." Um, and then at
zh: 然後當我在第二轉換區繞過碼頭跑出來時，我心想感覺不太好，但畢竟剛騎了112英里，這時候感覺不好也正常。接著我記得跑上轉換區出來的那個坡，在你右轉之前的第一條路上，我想應該是沿著市場街轉到阿里希大道。沿著那段直路，我的小腿突然感到一陣劇烈的疼痛，我心想：「糟了，這真的非常、非常嚴重。」
speaker: Guest

en: about 5K into the run, I finally saw Ree and I think I was just said to him, "It's bad. It's really bad." But didn't elaborate on that. And he was like pure panic at that point. What do you mean it's really bad? What's really bad?
zh: 大概跑到五公里的時候，我終於看到了Ree，我只對他說了一句：「很糟，真的很糟。」但沒有多做解釋。他當時完全慌了，一直問：「什麼意思很糟？到底什麼很糟？」
speaker: Guest

en: Um, and yeah, I think at that point he was trying to follow me for most of the marathon to to see what was going on. But even he was looking he was like, "I couldn't actually see any difference in your stride or the way that you were running. He wasn't compensating. He wasn't doing anything." So I think a lot of people maybe didn't actually know what was going on. Um obviously internally I was having quite a extreme battle but um yeah it was really really tough.
zh: 我想那時候他一直試著在馬拉松賽道上跟著我，想看看到底怎麼了。但即使他一直觀察，他也說：「我其實看不出你的步伐或跑姿有任何異常，你沒有代償，什麼都沒有。」所以我覺得很多人可能根本不知道發生了什麼事。顯然在內心裡，我正經歷一場非常激烈的掙扎，那真的非常、非常艱難。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Yeah I mean it's it's a long way right and Alihi on your way out you got to come back past the transition. You got to come past past everybody really knowing what what kind of a big day it is. um when you finally reach the finish line. Um you finally get that victory, you're in pain at the same time, but what was the actual feeling? Was it elation? Were you just in that moment happy that it was over? And what did that victory finally mean? Like where did it put you in terms of, you know,
zh: 嗯，我的意思是，那真的是一段很長的路，對吧？在阿利希那段路上，你回程的時候得再經過轉換區，得從所有人面前跑過去，心裡很清楚這是多麼重大的一天。嗯，當你終於抵達終點線的時候，你終於拿下了那場勝利，同時身體也承受著巨大的痛苦。但那一刻真正的感受是什麼？是狂喜嗎？還是你當下只是慶幸一切終於結束了？而那場勝利最終意味著什麼？就是說，它把你推到了什麼樣的位置，你知道的——
speaker: Host

en: your scope of of wanting to be an athlete?
zh: 你想成為運動員的目標範圍？
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah, it was such a mix of emotions. So, I think the biggest one I felt was relief that I'd finally done it and I felt like I had the monkey off my back. It definitely for a long time I'd had this pent up frustration and anger in my training of like I just need to do this and it just felt in that moment that was instantly lifted like I can breathe. I've done it. Like take a breath. Um and
zh: 是的，那真是百感交集。我覺得最強烈的感受是如釋重負，終於做到了，感覺卸下了心頭的大石。很長一段時間以來，我在訓練中一直壓抑著挫折感和憤怒，覺得「我必須做到這件事」。在那一刻，這一切瞬間被釋放了，像是終於可以呼吸了。我做到了，深呼一口氣。
speaker: Guest

en: then on the other side of that I was in so much pain like I knew I must have done something pretty bad. Um, and I just wanted to be able to like walk around normally, get up onto the podium and celebrate. And I was like having to get people help me onto the podium and
zh: 但另一方面，我痛得不得了，我知道自己肯定傷得很嚴重。我只想能正常走路、上領獎台好好慶祝。結果我需要別人攙扶才能上領獎台，
speaker: Guest

en: still try and enjoy that moment as much as possible, like take it all in. I think sometimes that's really difficult. You've obviously got so much adrenaline. You've been running on caffeine all day and you're just like, just try and absorb this moment. Soak it all in because you've been working for it for like seven, eight years now and it's finally happened. Um, but yeah, relief was the biggest thing I felt in that moment.
zh: 仍然試著盡可能享受那個時刻，把一切都吸收進來。我覺得有時候那真的很困難。你體內有那麼多腎上腺素，你一整天都靠咖啡因撐著，然後你就想：試著好好感受這一刻吧，把它全部吸收進來，因為你已經為此努力了七、八年，終於實現了。但是，對，在那個時刻我最大的感受就是如釋重負。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Yeah, arguably more than seven or eight years cuz you got to count your swimming years and everything that's built up to it. I mean, it's it's a long way.
zh: 是啊，其實不止七、八年，因為你還得算上游泳的那些年，以及所有累積起來的一切。我是說，這是一段很長的路。
speaker: Host

en: Interesting. You mentioned anger and frustration being there. I feel like that's part of an athlete life that doesn't really get shown. It's kind of one of those things that's frowned upon, like having a temper and all these kind of things. What do you do to actually channel that? like I found that was such an unlock in in my career, but I'd love to know how, you know, like I was saying at the top of the show, you're always wearing a smile, you're happy, and like I think you generally are, but there is a part, I think, in every athlete where
zh: 有趣。你提到了憤怒和挫折感的存在。我覺得這是運動員生活中很少被展現出來的一面。這算是那種不太被認可的事情，像是脾氣暴躁之類的。你實際上是怎麼去疏導這些情緒的？我發現這在我的職業生涯中是一個很大的突破，但我很想知道你是怎麼做的。就像我在節目開頭說的，你總是面帶微笑、開開心心的，我覺得你大體上確實是這樣的人，但我認為每個運動員心裡都有一部分是——
speaker: Host

en: you have this kind of builtup thing that you just want to crush a workout, crush the competition, and yet outwardly you carry yourself so well. H how do you how do you channel that and turn it into something positive?
zh: 你內心有這種積累的能量，就是想要拼盡全力完成訓練、徹底擊敗對手，但外在表現上你又總是那麼從容自若。你是怎麼引導這股力量，把它轉化為正面的能量的？
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah, it's not always easy to channel it in the right way. I think it's it's great if you can channel it into your workout into the intervals that you know are going to be really really tough. It's like right I know why I'm working for this because I want to achieve this or someone said this and it's really annoyed me so I'm going to channel that energy into just pushing the bike pedals
zh: 是的，要把它引導到正確的方向並不總是容易的。我覺得如果你能把它引導到訓練中，引導到那些你知道會非常非常艱難的間歇訓練中，那就太好了。就像是：好，我知道我為什麼要做這些，因為我想達成某個目標，或者某人說了什麼話真的惹怒我了，所以我要把那股能量導入踩踏板的力量中。
speaker: Guest

en: as hard as I can. And I think that works in a really positive way when it's a hard workout and when you're supposed to do that. it doesn't work so well when it leaks out into your normal everyday life and you're kind of then annoyed at your family or you're just a bit more snappy with Reese or things like that. So, it's just making sure that you're able to push it out in the right way. I think if
zh: 盡全力踩踏板。我覺得當你在進行高強度訓練、本來就該全力以赴的時候，這種方式非常有效。但當這種情緒滲透到你的日常生活中，你開始對家人發脾氣，或者對Reese比較容易不耐煩之類的，那就不太好了。所以重點是確保你能用正確的方式釋放這些情緒。
speaker: Guest

en: I have a if well I'm very different now I think but back then I would be able to have a really great workout and then I'd be happy for the rest of the day but if I had a really bad workout it could leak out into my normal life for the rest of the day. But I think as the years have gone on and maybe I'm really satisfied with what I have achieved in the sport
zh: 我覺得現在的我已經很不一樣了，但當時如果我有一次很棒的訓練，我接下來一整天都會很開心；但如果訓練很糟糕，那種負面情緒就會滲透到我一整天的日常生活中。但隨著這些年過去，也許是因為我對自己在這項運動中取得的成就已經很滿足了，
speaker: Guest

en: now, I can have a terrible workout, step off the bike and I don't care. It's like, okay, I'm going to go take my dogs for a walk and just forget about that. I can really park it. But it is really difficult when you really really want something and it just feels like you're putting everything in and you're just not quite achieving it. It is a challenge to not let that always have
zh: 現在我可以有一次很糟的訓練，下了車也完全不在意。就像：好吧，我去遛狗，把那件事忘掉就好。我真的能把它放下。但當你非常非常渴望某件事，感覺自己已經全力付出卻還是差一點達不到目標時，要不讓自己一直累積那種壓抑的憤怒，確實是很大的挑戰。
speaker: Guest

en: this pent up anger. I remember back then I used to swim quite a lot in the public lanes at the pool which is never a good idea for me but like someone would get in my way and then I'd be annoyed. It was like if anything was stopping me from doing my workout I'd flip out.
zh: 我記得那時候我常常在公共泳道游泳，對我來說那從來就不是個好主意。有人擋了我的路，我就會生氣。只要有任何事阻礙我完成訓練，我就會抓狂。
speaker: Guest

en: Whereas now I'm much more chill about that. I mean I don't swim in the public anymore. That's probably the >> that's probably the solution.
zh: 不過現在我淡定多了。我的意思是，我已經不在公共泳池游了。那大概就是——（主持人：那大概就是解決方法吧。）
speaker: Guest

en: >> But um yeah, just making sure that you can channel that energy into your workout but don't let it leak into your normal life.
zh: 但是，對，就是確保你能把那股能量導入訓練中，但不要讓它滲透到你的日常生活裡。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Yeah. I mean, I I find it's such an emotional thing, right? And it's so hard to step away and really not value yourself on your last workout, on your last race. I mean, that kind of thing is something I think that you've really mastered over the times, and you alluded to it earlier, how you've always picked the hard thing to do. And I was
zh: 是啊，我覺得這真的是一件非常情緒化的事情，對吧？要抽離出來，不用上一次訓練或上一場比賽的表現來衡量自己的價值，真的很難。我覺得你在這方面已經真正掌握了，你剛才也提到過，你總是選擇做困難的事情。
speaker: Host

en: listening to a neuroscience coach saying how you rewire your brain um by by evidence really you show yourself you can do hard things but let's start at the beginning what do you think made you drawn to do hard things like I always wonder why we didn't just become 100 me sprinters I mean genetically there is there's something to be asked but you could strive for it did you just naturally see that you had success in it or What really drove you to always look to do the hard thing and why your brain
zh: 聽一位神經科學教練說，你如何重新改寫你的大腦——其實是透過實證，你向自己證明你能做困難的事。但讓我們從頭開始，你覺得是什麼吸引你去做困難的事？我總是好奇，為什麼我們沒有乾脆去當百米短跑選手？我的意思是，從基因角度來看，這確實值得探討，但你可以朝那個方向努力。你是自然而然發現自己在這方面有天賦，還是到底是什麼驅使你總是去挑戰困難的事？還有，為什麼你的大腦會這樣？
speaker: Host

en: from, you know, singledigit years?
zh: 從，你知道的，個位數歲的時候？
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah, I feel like I came out of the womb just really competitive like always wanted to win at everything. Um, I have a younger sister and anything we did together, I wanted to win and then I think it led her to like I'm not even doing sport cuz I just  don't want to be involved in that world.
zh: 是的，我覺得我一出生就非常好勝，什麼事都想贏。我有一個妹妹，我們一起做任何事我都想贏，後來她大概就覺得：我根本不要碰運動了，我就是不想參與那個世界。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Became an artist. >> Yeah, she did. Oh, that's fair play to her. she is running her first marathon in just under two weeks. So, that's really exciting. But, um, yeah, growing
zh: （主持人：所以她成了藝術家。）是啊，確實。不過她真的很棒，她再不到兩週就要跑她人生第一場馬拉松了，真的很令人興奮。但是，對，成長過程中——
speaker: Guest

en: up, I think I must have been the really annoying sibling that just wanted to win at everything. And we learned over the years that my parents are quite competitive as well. Like, they'll go running together and my mom always wants to beat my dad. And yeah, I must have obviously had that built within me. But
zh: 我覺得小時候我一定是那種很煩人的兄弟姊妹，什麼都想贏。這些年來我們發現我爸媽也很好勝。他們會一起去跑步，我媽總是想贏過我爸。所以我肯定是天生就有這種特質。
speaker: Guest

en: from a very young age when I was training, I wanted to be the hardest worker. I wanted to be the person that was doing the most. And even in swimming, you'd have the people that their event was the 50 meter freestyle. Like they barely had to swim any mileage. They yes, they probably did a bit more work in the gym, but I'd be plowing up and down and they'd be on the side having their 3 minutes rest. And
zh: 從很小的時候開始訓練，我就想成為最努力的人。我想成為付出最多的人。在游泳的時候，有些人的項目是五十公尺自由式，他們根本不需要游多少距離。是的，他們可能在健身房多做了一些訓練，但我在水裡來回拼命游，他們卻在旁邊休息三分鐘。
speaker: Guest

en: but I was satisfied with that cuz I was like, I'm working harder than them. I'm doing more mileage. I'm putting in the work. I'm doing more hours. And I felt like I got rewarded and satisfied just from the training. And I feel like that now actually I can get such a buzz from doing a hard workout. It's almost the same as winning a race. That kind of elation and buzz you get from it. So yeah, I think that's probably fueled me from a very young age, that satisfaction of of working really hard.
zh: 但我對此感到滿足，因為我覺得我比他們更努力，我游了更多的距離，我付出了更多，花了更多的時間。我覺得光是訓練本身就能讓我獲得獎勵和滿足感。現在我也是這樣，完成一次高強度訓練帶來的興奮感，幾乎跟贏得比賽一樣。那種喜悅和快感。所以我覺得從小到大，努力工作帶來的滿足感一直是驅動我的動力。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Do you think that's a safety blanket?
zh: 你覺得那是一種安全感嗎？
speaker: Host

en: >> It probably is. Like I think it it's maybe a bit scary to think like if I don't do this anymore, where am I going to get that satisfaction from? And I can't ever see a life where I'm not exercising or going out and trying to Okay, I probably would have to accept that it might might not be trying to reach the levels that I'm at now, but finding a way of still challenging myself in a different way. I guess that might be running further or swimming further or Yeah, I don't really know.
zh: 應該是吧。我覺得想到「如果我不再做這件事了，我要從哪裡獲得那種滿足感？」確實有點嚇人。而且我無法想像一種完全不運動、不出去挑戰自己的生活。好吧，我大概必須接受，未來可能無法再追求我現在達到的這種水準，但要找到一種不同的方式來繼續挑戰自己。我想那可能是跑更遠的距離，或是游更長的距離，或者……嗯，我其實也不太確定。
speaker: Guest

en: But still finding a way to get that satisfaction from pushing myself.
zh: 但仍然找到一種方式，從挑戰自我中獲得那份成就感。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Yeah. I always find the thing is that you're continuously moving. we're always like seeking to do more and I I certainly found absolute comfort in always thinking I was the hardest worker and recently through this project I've gotten to speak to some athletes who are you know very explosive um a long jumper who does a threehour session and we'll do six intervals in 3 hours and I'm like she's the very best in the world I could never ever have the patience to sit there and not feel like,
zh: 對啊。我一直覺得問題就在於你會不斷地動個不停。我們總是想要做更多，而我自己確實從「我是最努力的人」這個想法中獲得了極大的安慰感。最近透過這個專案，我有機會跟一些運動員交流，他們都是那種非常具有爆發力的選手——比如一位跳遠選手，她一次訓練三個小時，但在這三個小時裡只做六組間歇訓練。我心想，她可是世界頂尖的選手，但換作是我，我絕對沒有那個耐心坐在那裡，而不會覺得——
speaker: Host

en: you know, because it actually takes some sort of confidence to be like, "No, this is enough. I need to rest now and push."
zh: 你知道嗎，因為要能夠說出「不，這樣就夠了，我現在需要休息，需要放過自己」，其實是需要某種自信的。
speaker: Host

en: Is that something that you're getting better at or is that something where you perhaps also get some help from Ree?
zh: 這方面你有在進步嗎？還是說你也會從 Ree 那裡得到一些幫助？
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah, I'm definitely not the most patient, that's for sure. And it's like when I think as you get older as an athlete, you have to spend more time doing the little things that hold everything together. So spending 15 20 minutes in the morning doing my mobility work so that when I go out and run I actually do feel good and before I'd be like no I haven't got time for that. I need to go and smash my workout. So it's being a bit more patient and accepting that. I also think that I used to like
zh: 是的，我絕對不是最有耐心的人，那是肯定的。我覺得隨著年齡增長，作為運動員你必須花更多時間在那些維持身體狀態的小事上。比如早上花十五到二十分鐘做活動度訓練，這樣出去跑步時才會感覺好。以前我會想：「不行，我沒時間做那個，我得趕快去衝訓練。」所以這就是要更有耐心、去接受這些事。我以前也覺得——
speaker: Guest

en: to just have all my training done for the day so I can chill but then there wasn't enough rest between the sessions. So, it's also being patient like, "No, I'm going to wait another hour before I do that workout so I can actually do it properly."
zh: 以前我總想把一天的訓練全部做完，這樣就可以放鬆了，但這樣各訓練之間的休息就不夠。所以這也是需要耐心的地方，告訴自己：「不，我要再等一個小時再做下一組訓練，這樣才能真正做到位。」
speaker: Guest

en: >> And yeah, that can be a challenge sometimes, especially when you're quite tired. You're like, I just rather have it done so that I can know I've done it and I can go and relax. But yeah, finding that patience can be a challenge. I definitely think I'm getting better at it. But I wouldn't say I'm perfect by any means.
zh: 沒錯，這有時確實是個挑戰，特別是當你很累的時候，你會想：我寧願趕快做完，這樣我就知道完成了，可以去放鬆。但是，要找到那份耐心確實不容易。我覺得自己在這方面有進步，但絕對算不上完美。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Yeah, it's forever a balance. I I do remember the kind of athletes who are always balancing like trying to get it done bit by bit versus the procra procrastination um and and and waiting out for it. And for me, it was always one of those things because I put so much heart and soul into every session. And I was not like you that I could step away and be fine with it. If I'm very honest, that maybe at the very end of my career, but
zh: 是啊，這永遠是一種平衡。我記得運動員們總是在試圖一點一點完成訓練和拖延之間掙扎。對我來說也一直是這樣，因為我在每一次訓練中都傾注了全部心血。我不像你那樣能夠抽離出來然後坦然面對。說實話，也許在我職業生涯的最後階段才做到。
speaker: Host

en: if we compare, you're you're a year younger than I was when I won my first world title. Like I feel like you've got your whole career ahead of you and you're already, you know, so much further down the line. But I definitely found that struggle to almost judge myself on on every session so much harder than going out and just, you know, getting it done and then focusing on the next day. Um,
zh: 但如果拿來比較的話，你現在的年紀比我贏得第一個世界冠軍時還小一歲。我覺得你的整個職業生涯還在前方，而你已經走得比當時的我遠得多了。但我確實發現，比起單純地完成訓練然後專注於第二天，用每一次訓練來評判自己要困難得多。
speaker: Host

en: what do you do to relax between sessions? What do you do to get ready for the next one? What do you how do how do you focus yourself? Yeah, this is also something I think I'm quite bad at because or even if I have like a down day or a rest day, I'm like, "All right, well, now I can maximize getting all the other stuff done in my life that's piled up that I haven't got done." So then it's like, "Oh, that wasn't really a rest day, was it?" Cuz I've spent all day doing admin and chores and sorting out the house. So that's something that I've also had to get better at. It's just parking that and be like, "No, I literally just need to go and sit down,
zh: 你在兩場比賽之間會做什麼來放鬆？你怎麼為下一場做準備？你怎麼讓自己集中注意力？嗯，這也是我覺得自己做得很差的地方，因為就算我有空檔的一天或休息日，我會想：「好吧，現在我可以把生活中堆積的、還沒處理完的其他事情全部搞定。」結果就變成：「噢，那其實根本不算休息日吧？」因為我一整天都在處理行政雜事、做家務、整理房子。所以這也是我一直在努力改進的地方，就是把那些事情先放到一邊，然後告訴自己：「不行，我真的就是需要去坐下來，
speaker: Guest

en: rest, maybe put some compression boots on." Or if I really need to switch off
zh: 休息一下，也許穿上氣壓式腿部按摩靴。」或者如果我真的需要好好放空一下
speaker: Guest

en: my mind, then obviously go and take the dogs for a walk is my absolute kind of therapy. I think me and Reese always put the world to right when we go for a dog walk and come back feeling so much better. So, they're definitely the best thing in terms of my recovery. But I think there's nothing better than just actually making yourself sit down, put your feet up, obviously eating good nutrition as soon as you can after a workout. I've I've definitely got better at that over the years as well. But
zh: 或者如果我真的需要讓大腦放空，那就去遛狗，那絕對是我的療癒方式。我覺得我和 Reese 每次遛狗的時候都能把所有煩心事聊開，回來後感覺好多了。所以狗狗們絕對是我恢復的最佳夥伴。但我覺得沒有什麼比真正讓自己坐下來、把腳抬起來更好的了，當然還有訓練後盡快補充營養。這些年我在這方面也進步了不少。
speaker: Guest

en: yeah, I think sometimes as athletes, it's allowing there to be a certain amount of chaos in your life outside of triathlon so that triathlon can thrive. And it's not always easy to to let that be going on on the outside.
zh: 但我覺得，作為運動員，有時候需要允許鐵人三項以外的生活有一定程度的混亂，這樣鐵人三項的訓練才能蓬勃發展。要接受外面的事情不完美，這並不總是容易的。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Yeah. I mean, it's one of those things how it's just so all consuming. And for you, I I don't know at what stage you
zh: 是啊，我是說，這就是其中一個難題——鐵人三項是多麼地消耗一切。而對你來說，我不知道你是在哪個階段……
speaker: Host

en: got it, but you have an Iron Man tattoo, right? >> Yeah, I got it when I was 21.
zh: 不知道你是什麼時候刺的，但你有一個鐵人賽的刺青，對吧？對，我21歲的時候刺的。
speaker: Host

en: >> Okay, there you go. So, you've had it >> before turning pro, too, right? You turned pro at >> Yeah, I I got  it when I did my first IM man as an amateur, never thinking I would be a pro at it. So,
zh: 好的，所以你在轉職業之前就有了，對吧？對，我第一次以業餘身份參加鐵人賽的時候就刺了，從來沒想過自己會成為職業選手。
speaker: Host

en: >> let alone You might be the first world champion to have one. um and and work your way there. Is that something where it gave you a new home? It gave you a purpose after swimming and after your early, you know, your early sports career that you really found yourself having an identity that to this day is kind of all consuming. You know,
zh: 更別說你可能是第一位有鐵人刺青的世界冠軍了。一路走到那裡，這是不是給了你一個新的歸屬？在游泳之後、在你早期的運動生涯之後，它給了你一個目標，讓你真正找到了一種身份認同，而這種認同至今仍然佔據了你的全部。
speaker: Host

en: you just said it, you've got time for a dog walk. Uh and and really other than that it's just triathlon, triathon, triathlon. Um, is is it something that really is all consuming? Because, you know, you seem like somebody who could enjoy life.
zh: 你剛才也說了，你只有時間遛狗，除此之外就是鐵人三項、鐵人三項、鐵人三項。這真的是全身心投入嗎？因為你看起來像是一個很懂得享受生活的人。
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah, I think that's one of the hardest things. I think it's also hard. I live on the edge of London and there knowing how many amazing things there are to do in the city that I could just go and do them whenever I want, but I don't. I kind of just ignore the fact that that's all there and try and get the most out of myself in training, but also trying to find as much enjoyment from the training itself. Like I think when when it's going well, I can really enjoy it.

en: I can enjoy the suffering. I can come away from a session like I said almost with the same feeling as if I've won a race knowing, wow, I just put in a amazing amount of work there. zh: >> 對，我覺得很早期的時候，我和 Ree 就討論過這件事。當我們開始做 YouTube 的時候，他就說：你現在真的是打開了潘朵拉的盒子。人們會覺得自己參與了你的生活，覺得他們了解你。我們確實討論過，到底要分享多少？就算看起來我們分享了很多，但大家真正了解的，大概只有真實的我的百分之五到百分之十而已。我會遇到一些人，覺得他們瞭解我的整個人生故事。那是因為我確實在網路上放了太多東西。所以，嗯，我想這就是這件事的福與禍吧——
speaker: Guest

en: sharing your story to people. And it's

speaker: Guest

en: super easy to share it when it's all going great. And yeah, when Holly's filming sessions that are going great and when I'm healthy, it's amazing. zh: 當一切進展順利的時候，分享是非常容易的。當Holly拍攝進展順利的訓練、當我很健康的時候，那真的很棒。但是當你記錄從傷病中復出的過程，人們對你為什麼受傷有各種各樣的看法——社群媒體可以是一個相當殘酷的世界。我在受傷的時候絕對經歷過這些，人們覺得他們完全知道是怎麼回事。
speaker: Guest

en: the really nice thing about my sister doing my social media is I can just say, "I've had enough. Stop filming me." Like, I don't want to be filmed today. When it's an external brand coming in, that's a little bit more difficult to do that. And then you also feel like you're not being your true honest self because you're being the best version of yourself for the sponsor that's filming you. But when it's my sister, I feel like actually sometimes maybe I'm too open and too honest and I shouldn't be sharing that with the world. So we have to yeah review how much of that is actually going to go out into the world. So yeah it's finding the right balance between what you're
zh: 我姐姐（妹妹）幫我經營社群媒體真正好的地方在於，我可以直接說：「我受夠了，別再拍我了。」就是，我今天不想被拍。但如果是外部品牌團隊來拍攝的話，要這樣說就比較困難了。而且你也會覺得自己沒辦法展現真實的一面，因為你會在贊助商的鏡頭前表現出最好的樣子。但如果是我姐姐（妹妹）拍的話，我覺得有時候我反而太開放、太坦誠了，有些東西其實不應該分享給全世界看。所以我們確實需要審視一下，哪些內容才適合真正發布出去。所以嘛，就是要在你願意分享的部分之間找到一個適當的平衡。
speaker: Guest

en: comfortable with and then actually but even if I'm comfortable with that is that still too much? So yeah.

I I

speaker: Guest

en: worthwhile. Yeah, I mean it it is something that I certainly later in my career found as something really really rewarding, right? zh: 我記得游泳的時候感覺不太好，但我做到了平常的水準，拉開了差距。所以我想：「也許沒事，可以忽略那種感覺。」我絕對是那種很擅長忽視身體不適的運動員，會跟自己說：「沒事的，狀態會回來的，再吃一包能量膠就好了，來點咖啡因，你會感覺很棒的。」然後我記得上了自行車後感到無比口渴。

en: Like I was like, "Wow, I've never felt this thirst in my life." And within the first, I don't know, 20 minutes on the bike, I'd got through two bottles of fluid and was like, "I'm still thirsty. zh: 我心想：「哇，我這輩子從來沒這麼渴過。」在上自行車的頭二十分鐘內，我就喝完了兩瓶水，但還是覺得渴，心想到底怎麼回事。然後就不停地從補給站拿水瓶，盡可能地猛灌。
speaker: Guest

en: made was that then I would be adding electrolyte tablets too quickly to my bottle when I'm only meant to have one an hour. So then when I got onto

speaker: Guest

en: this planet. zh: 完全不在狀態。所以後來回家之後，我跟醫生和專家討論了這一切，我們做了血液檢查，也檢視了各項指標。我還跟一位熱壓力專科醫生談過，他是倫敦馬拉松的菁英運動員隊醫，專門研究其他運動員的中暑症狀，在這方面有非常豐富的知識。
speaker: Guest

en: I really don't think you had heat stroke. zh: 他說：「我真的不認為你是中暑。我認為你是低血鈉症，你攝取了過多的鹽分，然後又喝了太多白開水過度補水，基本上你的身體就像一個化學反應失控的試管。從那個時間點開始，一切就不可能恢復正常了。」
speaker: Guest

en: think that's why I made such a quick recovery because um yeah, I was able to come around from that so much quicker than if it had been a heat stress injury. zh: 這就讓我回到最一開始我們談到的，你知道的，關於內在和外在目標的部分。你已經說過很多次，你仍在進步，你的數據越來越好，你還沒有達到你的天花板，而且在某種程度上，你其實很好奇，在沒有小腿撕裂傷的情況下，你到底能表現到多好。嗯，你要怎麼在——你知道的——可能達到你個人最佳成績之間找到平衡
speaker: Host

en: numbers and race, but really tying that to an outcome? I always found that very difficult as an athlete.
zh: 數字和比賽，但真的要把那些跟結果連結起來？我身為運動員時，總覺得那非常困難。
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah, it it is really hard because you might do your best ever numbers and your best performance, but you still might not win and then are you satisfied? That that would be quite hard, I think. But then on the flip side of that, you could definitely win the race and know that you had more, which is I guess where I'm sat right now. And
zh: 是的，這真的很難，因為你可能跑出史上最好的數據和最佳表現，但你仍然可能無法贏，那你會滿意嗎？我覺得那會很難接受。但反過來說，你也可能贏得比賽，但知道自己還有更多潛力沒發揮出來，我想這就是我現在所處的位置。
speaker: Guest

en: >> me and Reese actually have this saying which I think comes from possibly 2019 when you won in Kona and you just looked so superior running the other way on the Queen K as I run out. And we always say, you can't retire until you've had that Yan moment where you  win. And it looked so superior. Obviously, I don't know what was going on in your head. I don't know how you felt, but from the outside, it just looked like no one was ever going to beat you on that day because you were just superior compared to everyone else. So, yeah, I'd
zh: 我和Reese之間其實有句口頭禪，我覺得應該是源自2019年你在Kona奪冠的時候。當時我正跑出去，而你在Queen K公路的對向跑回來，看起來就是遙遙領先、無人能敵。我們總是說：「在你擁有屬於自己的『Yan時刻』之前，你不能退役。」就是那種贏得比賽、看起來完全凌駕所有人的時刻。當然，我不知道當時你腦子裡在想什麼，也不知道你的感受如何，但從外面看來，那天就是沒有人能打敗你，因為你跟其他所有人比起來就是高出一截。所以，嗯，我會說——
speaker: Guest

en: like to try and feel like I'm having my moment like that in Kona and then I would be satisfied.
zh: 我想嘗試在科納也能擁有那樣屬於我的輝煌時刻，那樣我就心滿意足了。
speaker: Guest

en: It really is something that Yeah, I guess you you've built and that you've fought after. I do kind of think like what did you take from that win that all the difficult moments, the setbacks, the I don't even want to say bad races cuz they weren't bad races. You know, if you're second in the world, it's not a bad race. But in our mind, because I know you well enough to know that we probably think in a
zh: 這真的是一件，嗯，我想是你一手打造、努力爭取來的成就。我確實在想，從那場勝利中，你學到了什麼？那些艱難的時刻、那些挫折、那些——我甚至不想說是糟糕的比賽，因為那些根本不算糟糕。你知道的，如果你拿到世界第二，那不叫糟糕的比賽。但在我們心裡，因為我夠了解你，知道我們大概都會用一種——
speaker: Host

en: similar vein and that, you'd look at it as a setback. And what do you think the win the setbacks taught you that the win never could? I mean, I've I've kind of
zh: 類似的情況，你會把它視為一次挫折。那你認為那些挫折教會了你什麼，是勝利永遠無法教你的？我的意思是，我大概
speaker: Host

en: fought this before, but if id rocked up to Kona in 2017 and just won and not come second at that early point in my career, I probably wouldn't have stayed in the sport of triathlon because I would have been like, "Job done, completed it. What's the next challenge?" Whereas having to have those challenges over the years, the amount that allowed me to grow and become a better athlete to dive deeper into how I can be better in terms of aerodynamics, nutrition, biomechanics, strength and conditioning, like I maybe wouldn't have ever tapped into those areas. So, I
zh: 我之前也曾經有過這樣的掙扎，但如果我在2017年就直接去科納拿了冠軍，而不是在職業生涯那麼早期只拿到第二名的話，我可能根本不會留在鐵人三項這個運動裡，因為我會覺得：「任務完成了，搞定了。下一個挑戰是什麼？」然而，這些年來經歷的那些挑戰，讓我有了巨大的成長空間，讓我成為更好的運動員，讓我更深入地去鑽研如何在空氣動力學、營養學、生物力學、體能訓練等各方面變得更強——如果不是這樣，我可能永遠不會去探索這些領域。所以，我
speaker: Guest

en: wouldn't have grown and become better. But then the mental strength to keep
zh: 就不會成長和變得更好。但接著，要保持下去的心理韌性
speaker: Guest

en: coming back when you know you got better but you still didn't win. Someone else still beat you and you're like right well what can I tap into now to find those extra sense to be better. Then to do that four times and still be second and still be like no I still want this. I'm still going to come back. I'm still too stubborn to step away and be
zh: 但你還需要心理上的韌性，不斷地回來——明明知道自己已經進步了，卻還是沒贏。別人還是打敗了你，然後你就想：好吧，我現在還能從哪裡挖掘潛力，再進步一點？然後你這樣做了四次，還是第二名，但你仍然覺得：不，我還是想要這個。我還要回來。我太固執了，不願意就這樣放手、感到滿足。
speaker: Guest

en: satisfied. So then on the day when I did get the win for it to go so horribly wrong compared to all of those times before it's like it didn't match up. It was like but you won but it went wrong whereas all the times before it was almost nearly perfect and you were second. So it almost felt like the two things weren't aligned. And then
zh: 所以當我終於贏的那一天，比賽過程卻出了那麼多差錯，跟之前那些次相比完全不一樣。就好像兩件事對不上——你贏了，但過程出了問題；而之前那些次幾乎完美，你卻是第二名。所以感覺這兩件事根本不一致。
speaker: Guest

en: I guess that's why I'm like I want to go back and just deliver my perfect performance but that might not lead to a win. that might lead to a second like it did those four times before. Um, but it's just putting it all together and I think I have done that on the swim. I don't think I did that on the swim in 2023 because I've swam faster years before, but conditions are so dependent in the swim. So, I feel like my swim could still be faster. The bike was
zh: 我想這就是為什麼我想回去，交出一場完美的表現——但那可能不會帶來勝利，可能又會像之前四次一樣得第二名。但重點就是把所有事情整合在一起。我覺得我在游泳項目上已經做到了。我不認為2023年游泳是我最好的表現，因為我之前幾年游得更快，但游泳很受水況條件影響。所以我覺得我的游泳還能更快。
speaker: Guest

en: pretty good, but the conditions were pretty good. And I'm actually cycling. I will I believe by October this year I'll be cycling better than I was in 2023 and the run feels like well that easily should be better because as long as I don't tear my whole whole calf how could I be slower but that's still not not how it could play out because last year I didn't even finish the race. So yeah,
zh: 自行車表現不錯，但條件也很好。而且我的自行車實力——我相信到今年十月會比2023年更強。跑步的話，感覺應該很容易進步，因為只要我不撕裂整條小腿肌肉，怎麼可能更慢呢？但事情不一定會那樣發展，因為去年我連比賽都沒完成。
speaker: Guest

en: that's the beauty of sport. That's why we keep coming back and it's not like oh I'll submit all the training that I did and that means I'm going to get the gold medal here. Like it doesn't work like that. That's why we all watch and that's why we're all fans of the sport. So that's what keeps me excited about one, what I can achieve and two, who knows what the outcome will be even if I deliver my best performance.
zh: 所以這就是運動的魅力。這就是為什麼我們不斷回來，而不是說「我把所有訓練數據交上去，就能拿金牌」。運動不是這樣的。這就是為什麼我們都愛看比賽，都是這項運動的粉絲。所以讓我興奮的是：第一，我能達成什麼；第二，即使我發揮出最佳表現，結果仍然是未知數。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Of the things that you've just said that really sticks out to me, it's stubborn. You said you're stubborn and stubborn can be very good cuz you need to persist, right? because you talk about 4 seconds, but it's really only three because the first time you're second, it's like, holy. Yeah, like I'm here, you know, this is this is amazing. Uh it's the progress where where things kind of stay, but you stick with it.
zh: 你剛才說的那些話裡，最讓我印象深刻的一個詞是「固執」。你說你很固執，而固執可以是一件好事，因為你需要堅持不懈，對吧？你說了四次第二名，但其實真正算起來只有三次，因為第一次拿第二名時，你的感覺是：天哪，太棒了，我居然站在這裡了。真的是在進步的過程中，成績停滯了，但你堅持了下來。
speaker: Host

en: You're stubborn. You say you're suffering through sessions. You did not pull off the course. It was Reese that pulled you off the course. Um, for years you had you you were celiac, right? And you had stress fractures and low energy and all these things, but the idea that your body is telling you something for that long and you just push through it. Do you think every time
zh: 你很固執。你說你在訓練中硬撐。你並沒有自己退出賽道，是乃芮絲把你從賽道上拉下來的。嗯，好幾年來你一直有乳糜瀉，對吧？你有疲勞性骨折、體力不足，還有各種問題，但你的身體這麼長時間一直在向你發出警訊，你卻一直硬撐過去。你覺不覺得每一次——
speaker: Host

en: you learn a little bit or is it something that is just, you know, you got to take the good with the bad?
zh: 你會從中學到一點東西，還是說這就是那種，你知道的，好的壞的你都得一起接受？
speaker: Host

en: >> I think we're always learning. Like I I never think I'll stop learning in this in this game of triathlon, but also in this game of life and like learning
zh: 我覺得我們一直在學習。我從來不認為我會在鐵人三項這場比賽中停止學習，同樣地，在人生這場比賽中也是如此，學習是永無止境的。
speaker: Guest

en: about health and what's actually normal, like the what I felt like what I thought was just normal to feel like that. Like, oh, I'm just tired from the training that I've done or my joints are just in agony cuz I push that session so hard. I just kind of thought everyone must feel like that all the time or I'd go for a long run and my joints would hurt for the rest of the day. It wasn't until I
zh: 關於健康以及什麼才是真正的正常狀態。我以前覺得那些感覺都是正常的，比如「噢，我只是因為訓練太累了」，或者「我的關節痛到不行是因為那節訓練太拼了」。我以為每個人都一直是這種感覺，或者我去跑個長距離，關節就會痛一整天。
speaker: Guest

en: think last year when I was training for Kona and I was doing long runs and I was like I feel completely fine after doing this. Like is this what all of my competitors have felt like all the time? Whereas I'd have to go and lay down and be in agony. So yeah, I think finding out that I was celiac felt incredible.
zh: 直到去年我在為Kona備戰，跑完長距離後我發現自己感覺完全沒事，我就想：這就是我所有競爭對手一直以來的感受嗎？而我之前每次都得躺下來，痛苦不堪。所以，發現自己有乳糜瀉的那一刻感覺真的太棒了。
speaker: Guest

en: Like I was like, "Wow, this is going to be like my secret weapon now because I've spent all these years doing it the hard way. Surely now it's going to be easier. And okay, it might be like 1% easier, but half the time it's still really really tough. But the the recovery is what's so much better. And I
zh: 我當時想：「哇，這將成為我的秘密武器了，因為我這些年都是用最艱難的方式在撐，現在肯定會輕鬆一些了。」好吧，可能只是輕鬆了百分之一，大部分時候還是非常非常辛苦。但恢復能力確實好了太多。
speaker: Guest

en: think where the run was always my weakest discipline, it was probably because I my body just couldn't recover from the toll of running and poisoning myself and yeah, whatever I ate not being absorbed into my body. So yeah, that felt like a an amazing moment. And it also kind of felt like I was able to
zh: 我覺得跑步一直是我最弱的項目，可能就是因為我的身體根本無法從跑步的負荷中恢復過來——一邊跑步一邊在毒害自己，而且吃進去的東西也沒有被身體吸收。所以，那真的是一個很棒的時刻。而且那也讓我能夠做到另一件事——
speaker: Guest

en: then just silence all the people that had had opinions on why I was getting stress fractures all the time. Like I do remember I think when I got one of my really really bad injuries and I don't know if it was aimed at me but someone had posted something like bone injury simple eat more and I was like if that
zh: 讓那些一直對我為什麼總是應力性骨折發表意見的人閉嘴。我記得有一次我受了一次非常嚴重的傷，不知道是不是針對我的，但有人發了一條類似「骨傷很簡單，多吃點就好了」的東西。
speaker: Guest

en: was aimed at me like well I'm going to be mad because I knew that that wasn't the case in my situation. So, it was just really nice to get that confirmation and know that this is probably why that was all happening.
zh: 我當時想，如果那是針對我說的，我真的會很生氣，因為我知道在我的情況下根本不是那回事。所以，能得到那個確認，知道這可能就是一切問題的原因，真的讓人很欣慰。
speaker: Guest

en: >> It's amazing, isn't it? You're the best in the world of what you do and and somebody always has an opinion and somebody always knows better. But I do love how there how you manage to take spite in in in the best possible way and turn it into your fuel. There's always a little bit, you know, whether it's the lady next door in the in the lane next to you or or or the online coach. I
zh: 這真的很不可思議，對吧？你已經是世界上最頂尖的選手了，但總有人要發表意見，總有人覺得自己更懂。不過我很喜歡你能用最好的方式把那些不屑化為動力。總會有那麼一點點，不管是泳道隔壁的那位女士，還是網路上的教練。
speaker: Host

en: mean, Triathlon definitely has an amazing share of of armchair coaches that that we're definitely not shy of, but I guess people are just very heavily invested and they just they actually care and they actually think they have something to help you. It's just taking it on the right way, right?
zh: 我是說，鐵人三項界確實有大量的「沙發教練」，這方面我們真的不缺。但我想人們只是投入了很多感情，他們真的很在乎，也真心覺得自己能幫到你。關鍵就是用正確的方式去看待這些，對吧？
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah. I mean, some of it is great advice from time to time, but most of it is your armchair expert thinking they know best, but there's a lot of passionate fans in triathlon, which makes it what it is. It makes it such a great sport. But, um, yeah, you got to take the good with the bad sometimes.
zh: 對，有些建議偶爾確實不錯，但大部分都是沙發專家覺得自己最懂。不過鐵人三項有很多充滿熱情的粉絲，這也是這項運動之所以偉大的原因。但是，有時候你就得好的壞的都接受。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Do you sometimes just switch off and just not look at it at all?
zh: 你有時候會乾脆關掉社群媒體，完全不看嗎？
speaker: Host

en: >> Yeah, definitely. I think I think it was actually after Kona where I just I basically went into my bubble and was like, you've got four weeks until the
zh: 會，絕對會。我記得其實就是在Kona之後，我基本上就躲進了自己的小世界裡，心想你還有四週時間——
speaker: Guest

en: next World Championships. I didn't look at a single piece of social media. Like I I don't even know what what people said about what they thought happened because I just didn't look. I was like, I've had my what my doctor thinks happened. I'm just going to go with that and I'm just going to now go away and work and be very very quiet. And I think
zh: 距離下一場世錦賽只有四週。我完全沒有看任何社群媒體。我甚至不知道大家對發生的事情有什麼看法，因為我根本沒去看。我想，我的醫生已經告訴我他認為發生了什麼事，我就照著那個走就好。然後我就安安靜靜地去訓練。
speaker: Guest

en: that actually ended up being a big part of why I won that race because so many people had written me off and they didn't think that I would even be lining up whereas I was quietly training away and just inwardly focusing on myself and and how I could turn around that disappointment and it definitely paid off.
zh: 我覺得那其實成了我贏得那場比賽的一個重要原因，因為很多人已經把我排除在外，他們甚至不認為我會站上起跑線。而我卻在默默地訓練，專注於自己，思考如何扭轉那次的失望，結果證明這絕對是值得的。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Imagine how good you could have been if you listened to Chad and Karen. They would have probably
zh: 想像一下如果你當時聽了 Chad 和 Karen 的話，你可能會有多厲害。他們可能會……
speaker: Host

en: Lucy. Uh before I let you go, if you weren't a triathlete and genuinely you you you'd never found it, who do you what what do you think you'd be doing?
zh: Lucy，在我讓你離開之前，如果你不是鐵人三項選手，真的從來沒有接觸過這項運動，你覺得你會做什麼？
speaker: Host

en: >> Oh, I can't see a world where I wouldn't be in sport and when I first came into triathlon, I was doing personal training and I absolutely loved it. It was one of the hardest things I ever did was actually giving that up to pursue triathlon professionally. So yeah, I think it would be in some form of coaching or personal training. Just trying to improve people's lives in some way.
zh: 噢，我無法想像自己不在運動圈的世界。當我剛進入鐵人三項時，我在做私人教練，我非常熱愛那份工作。放棄那份工作去從事職業鐵人三項是我做過最困難的決定之一。所以，我覺得我會從事某種形式的教練或私人教練工作，就是想辦法改善人們的生活。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Love it. Love it. Um, one last thing I do have to ask you which I normally ask at the top of the show, but I actually forgot cuz we went straight into it. Um, when you hear going mental like that was mental, uh, what does that mean to you? Define the term going mental. For me,
zh: 很棒。我還有最後一個問題要問你，我通常會在節目開頭問，但我其實忘了，因為我們直接就聊起來了。當你聽到「going mental」——就是「那真是太瘋狂了」——這對你來說意味著什麼？請定義一下「going mental」這個詞。
speaker: Host

en: >> I think it's very positive in the world of sport anyway. If someone's like, "That was mental." I'm like, "Yes, I absolutely smashed it then." Like, I must be doing something that no one's ever done before. And I enjoy going mental in training sometimes. Like, when I see the coach's face, like, whoa, okay, we're on to something. Then, yeah, I'm like, yeah, we are going mental. And yeah, I thrive off that.
zh: 我覺得在運動界這是非常正面的詞。如果有人說「那真是太瘋狂了」，我會覺得「太好了，我一定是表現得超出預期了」。就像我一定做了前所未有的事情。我有時候在訓練中也喜歡「發瘋」。當我看到教練的表情像是「哇，好，我們有戲了」，我就會想「是的，我們正在全力以赴」。我真的很享受那種感覺。
speaker: Guest

en: >> When was the last time you went mental?
zh: 你上一次「發瘋」是什麼時候？
speaker: Host

en: >> I think I went mental most days last week, to be honest. what I'm doing in chaining at the moment is quite mental, but um hopefully it's going to lead to something good.
zh: 老實說，我覺得上週幾乎每天都在「發瘋」。我目前的訓練內容確實蠻瘋狂的，但希望能帶來好的結果。
speaker: Guest

en: >> Fantastic, Lucy. I always admire your resilience. I admire your spirit and I admire the smile you you bring to every raceourse. Uh thank you so much for your time. I just want to wish you the very best of luck. Fingers crossed for this afternoon's first run outside. That feeling is so unique. It actually gives me goosebumps. It's uh everybody who's been injured for for for many times knows how much expectation and hope goes into this and uh everyone here is cheering for you. So,
zh: 太棒了，Lucy。我一直很欽佩你的韌性，欽佩你的精神，也欽佩你在每個賽場上帶來的笑容。非常感謝你的時間。我想祝你一切順利，祝今天下午第一次戶外跑步順利。那種感覺真的非常獨特，讓我起雞皮疙瘩。每個曾經受過傷的人都知道，那裡面承載了多少期待和希望，這裡每個人都在為你加油。
speaker: Host

en: >> best of luck for that as a baby step to a great 2026.
zh: 祝這一小步成為邁向精彩 2026 年的開始，祝你好運。
speaker: Host

en: >> Thank you very  much.
zh: 非常感謝你。
speaker: Guest

en: Polit from pot stars by OM
zh: Podcast 之星的 Polit，由 OM 提供。
